heroHeadline: Min Aung Hlaing Sworn In as President — China and India Attend Inauguration Formalizing Junta's Civilian Transition
heroSubtitle: Myanmar junta chief Min Aung Hlaing was officially sworn in as the country's 11th president on April 10, with China's special envoy and India's Minister of State for External Affairs attending the ceremony — conferring regional legitimacy on the SAC's cosmetic civilianization project condemned by the NUG, UN, US, and EU. The inauguration completes months of constitutional theater: Min Aung Hlaing retired as C-in-C on March 30, was elected vice-president April 1, and was chosen president April 3 by the junta's packed electoral college. On April 7, SAC jets conducted seven bombing runs on an IDP displacement camp in Shwebo Township (Sagaing), killing a monk teaching displaced children — a stark contrast to the regime's nominal civilian transition. Sustained fighting in Minbu-Saku (Magway) and resistance ambushes in Mon State killed four SAC troops. The KIA's Hpakant offensive continued into April 8 with SAC shelling injuring civilians. On April 9, airstrikes continued against Wuntho Township villages as the junta prepared its inaugural ceremony.
footerNote: Data sourced from AAPP, ACLED, UN OCHA, Irrawaddy, RFA, Myanmar Now, DVB, Al Jazeera, Mizzima, Human Rights Watch, Crisis Group, Myanmar Peace Monitor, MoeMaKa, and official NUG, KNU, KIA, and AA statements.
